beats.n_start: first morning of summer and {THRESHOLD} finally opens its tall green doors to you, {ENTRY_TOKEN} in hand; inside smells of brass polish, sawdust, and glue; {GUILDMASTER} fits you with your own safety glasses and points out three workshop doors off the great hall, one {ROUTE_G_LURE}, one {ROUTE_L_LURE}, one {ROUTE_M_LURE} opening onto {STREET_NAME}
beats.n_tour: the tour winds through all three workshops: in the gear hall {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} polishes a model of the town square's silent four-season fountain clock; up on the roof {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} clips your harness to the {L_RAIL} and shows off the glider launch ramp and the pigeon {ROUTE_L_LOFT}; in the gadget shop {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} is mending a shopkeeper's bell and makes you ask the question twice; every mentor says the same thing, tomorrow is the entrance {ENTRY_TEST}
beats.n_test: the {ENTRY_TEST}: a box of odd parts, springs, dowels, a cracked pulley, one bent hinge, and one hour to make something that does something; your contraption wobbles, jams, gets rebuilt twice, and finally whirs across the table to polite cheers; {GUILDMASTER}, you notice, was not watching what you built, she was watching how you fixed it each time it failed
beats.n_sort: {GUILDMASTER} says the test proves you could thrive behind any of the three doors, so the choice is yours for the whole summer: the {ROUTE_G_NAME} workshop, under {ROUTE_G_MENTOR}, is {ROUTE_G_TASK} for {ROUTE_G_EVENT}; the {ROUTE_L_NAME} workshop, under {ROUTE_L_MENTOR}, is {ROUTE_L_TASK}; the {ROUTE_M_NAME} workshop, under {ROUTE_M_MENTOR}, is {ROUTE_M_TASK} before {ROUTE_M_EVENT}; three doors stand open
beats.g_a1: your first morning in the gear hall: a workbench with your name chalked on it, rows of tiny screwdrivers, and {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} peering through her magnifier glasses at you the way she inspects a gear, kindly but closely; safety glasses on before anything turns, she says
beats.g_a2: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} unveils the guild's model of the fountain clock: on every hour, four brass season figures, a {G_FIGURE_SPRING}, a {G_FIGURE_SUMMER}, an {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN}, a {G_FIGURE_WINTER}, should parade around the fountain rim while eight bells chime, all driven by a {G_WHEEL} under the square; the real clock has stood silent for twenty years, and the guild has promised the town it will run again at {ROUTE_G_EVENT}
beats.g_a3: {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}, a junior with two summers of grease under his nails, announces that the hard jobs are his and the toothbrush jobs are yours; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} ignores this and splits the restoration in two: someone must wake the four season figures, and someone must take on the {G_BELLS} and the {G_WHEEL} below the square
beats.g_b1: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} lays two work orders on your bench and lets you choose your summer: the season figures wait in the workshop, green with age, a puzzle of tiny springs and fused wings; the {G_BELLS} means the clock tower stairs and the flooded {G_WHEEL} cellar, ropes, ladders, and bells bigger than you are; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} mutters that he wants whichever one you do not take
beats.g_c1: the four figures stand on your bench like a tiny frozen parade: the {G_FIGURE_SPRING}'s wings jammed mid-beat, the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER} caught mid-leap, the {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN} hunched, the {G_FIGURE_WINTER}'s head locked staring backward; sixty years of grime hide every screw
beats.g_c2: the {G_FIGURE_SPRING}'s wing gears are fused into one green lump, and {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} leans over your shoulder to say you will strip the teeth if you force it; annoyingly, he is right, your first try barely avoids disaster
beats.g_c3: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} teaches you the slow cure: a vinegar bath, a soft toothbrush, and patience by the hour, and by the end of the week the {G_FIGURE_SPRING}'s wing gives one clean tick and half a wingbeat; two figures remain in real trouble, and {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} says each is a different kind of hard
beats.g_c4: the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER}'s great leap spring is snapped clean in two, which means forging and coiling a new one at the forge corner, hot work you have never done; the {G_FIGURE_WINTER}'s head is seized solid on a bent arbor deep inside its body, which means a careful teardown and a press; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} will let you lead on exactly one
beats.g_f1: the forge corner smells of coal and hot metal; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} shows you spring steel, how it must be coiled while cherry-red and cooled just so; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}, passing, says spring coiling took him a whole summer to learn
beats.g_f2: your first coil snaps like a biscuit, the second comes out lopsided, the third looks almost right; your wrist aches and the festival is getting closer on the wall calendar; the last step is tempering, and {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} says that is where springs are truly made or ruined
beats.g_f3: tempering is a choice between speed and certainty: quench the spring fast tonight and you could test the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER}'s leap tomorrow and still catch up on the other figures; or bank the coals and temper it slow overnight the way the old manuals say, spending days you barely have; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} has left, the forge is yours
beats.g_f4: the fast-quenched spring looks perfect, bright and eager, and the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER} takes one glorious test leap across the bench before a sharp ping says everything; the spring has shattered into glittering pieces, brittle from the hurry
beats.g_f5: the slow temper works: the new spring rings true when you tap it, and the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER} leaps; but one good spring leads to the {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN}'s tired springs, then the {G_FIGURE_SPRING}'s, and the summer quietly becomes a summer of springs
beats.g_g1: the {G_FIGURE_WINTER} comes apart screw by careful screw until you find the trouble: the arbor its head turns on is bent like a hairpin, and it will not come free without an arbor press; the guild owns exactly one
beats.g_g2: the press lives on {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s bench, and {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} names his price with a grin: help him finish the {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN} he is secretly behind on, or figure out your own way to pull a bent arbor; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR}, suspiciously busy nearby, says nothing at all
beats.g_g3: you sketch a makeshift press out of a bench vise, two clamps, and stubbornness, and it might just work if nothing slips; or you could take {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s deal, share the press, and spend your evenings on his {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN}, which means trusting the one person who calls your work toothbrush jobs; the {G_FIGURE_WINTER}'s backward stare is not helping
beats.g_g4: the vise-press holds, creaks, holds, and then slips exactly the way {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} said it would; the arbor jumps like a snapped twig and a crack runs through the {G_FIGURE_WINTER}'s crown gear, the one part nobody has a spare for
beats.g_g5: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} turns the cracked crown gear in the light for a long minute; a new one must be cast in the city, she says, and casting takes past {ROUTE_G_EVENT}; some jobs, she adds gently, are two-person jobs no matter how clever one person is
beats.g_h1: working with {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} turns out to be nothing like arguing with him: at the press he is careful, quick, and quietly funny, and the bent arbor slides free on the second pull; in trade you tune his {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN}'s hop until it lands like a feather
beats.g_h2: the {G_FIGURE_WINTER}'s head turns, the {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN} hops, the {G_FIGURE_SPRING} beats both wings, and the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER} leaps on {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s borrowed spring; then the basin test ruins everything: down at the fountain, the parade ring the figures ride on jams solid the moment the water flows over it
beats.g_h3: a night of cocoa and sketches at {ROUTE_G_MENTOR}'s big table turns up the answer in the oldest drawing: the parade ring was never meant to run underwater at all; there was once a {G_DRAIN_GATE} that lowered the basin for every parade, and it has been lost for decades
beats.g_h4: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} sees two honest paths to {ROUTE_G_EVENT}: run the parade with the basin drained dry, a sure thing the town would still love, and hunt the {G_DRAIN_GATE} at leisure afterward; or gamble the remaining weeks on finding and freeing the lost {G_DRAIN_GATE} so the seasons can parade over rushing water the way the makers meant; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} says he is in either way
beats.g_h5: you drain the basin, true up the dry ring, and rehearse the parade until all four figures run like sunrise; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} schedules a public dry-run reveal a week before {ROUTE_G_EVENT}, just to be safe
beats.g_h6: half the town shows up to the so-called small reveal; when the {G_FIGURE_SPRING} leads the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER}, {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN}, and {G_FIGURE_WINTER} around the dry rim for the first time in twenty years, the square goes quiet in the good way
beats.g_i1: the guild archives smell of dust and secrets; you and {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} find the gatekeeper's diagram folded inside a ledger from before your grandparents: the drain crank hides behind the fountain's little brass door, the one everyone always assumed was decoration
beats.g_i2: behind the brass door, under forty years of mud, the drain crank still turns; you clean it tooth by tooth while {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} rebuilds its chain, and at dawn on a Tuesday the basin empties in ninety glorious seconds, then fills again like magic
beats.g_i3: {ROUTE_G_EVENT} morning: bunting on every lamppost, the square packed shoulder to shoulder, the basin brimming; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} holds out the great {G_KEY}, which by guild custom belongs to the restorer's hand, while {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} waits at the brass door ready on the drain crank
beats.g_i4: one minute to noon and one honor to give away: take the {G_KEY} {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} is offering and set the seasons parading yourself while {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} works the hidden crank; or press the {G_KEY} into {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s hand, two summers earned, and take the crank behind the brass door, the job nobody sees and nothing works without
beats.g_i5: you set the {G_KEY}, brace, and wind; underground the drain thunders open on {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s crank, the water bows out of the basin, and the four seasons step out over the wet-shining ring exactly at noon
beats.g_i6: {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} stares at the {G_KEY} in his hand like it might be a joke, then grips it like it is treasure; you duck behind the brass door, hands on the crank, watching noon come through a slot the size of a letterbox
beats.g_j1: the clock tower stairs corkscrew up past the great silent bells, eight of them, each cast with a season vine; far below, a dripping cellar under the square holds the {G_WHEEL}, drowned in sixty years of dead leaves; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} gives you the tower key and one rule: never past the yellow rail without a rope-buddy
beats.g_j2: the survey is honest and grim: the {G_WHEEL} below is so clogged it cannot turn a spoon, and the {G_BELLS} above has a bent hammer, slipped cams, and one bell that only whispers; nothing rings and nothing turns, and {ROUTE_G_EVENT} is eight weeks out
beats.g_j3: {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} appears at the tower door with a bet: he can voice the bells sweeter than you can, any day; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} turns the bet into a plan, {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} may race you on bell voicing, but the timekeeping is yours alone, because a chime that rings sweet but late, she says, is just a pretty lie
beats.g_j4: two starts, one choice: begin below in the cellar, freeing the {G_WHEEL} that powers everything, cold muddy work that makes every later job possible; or begin above with the {G_BELLS} itself, mapping cams and hammers in the high bell chamber while {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} tunes and hums across the scaffold from you
beats.g_k1: the cellar {G_WHEEL} is taller than a door, furred with leaf-rot, and stuck fast; you bail, rake, and scrape for days, and when the axle finally groans a quarter-turn you cheer alone in the dark like it was {ROUTE_G_EVENT} already
beats.g_k2: freed is not fixed: three paddles are rotten right through, and the sluice that feeds the {G_WHEEL} will pound whatever you mount there; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} is up a scaffold all week, {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} is bell-deep in his bet, and the festival calendar keeps losing days; the paddle question is yours to settle
beats.g_k3: the quick way: patch the rotten paddles with tin sheet and clever folding, done in two days, probably fine, probably; the long way: fell, split, and carve proper oak paddles like the millwrights did, most of a summer's work for a {G_WHEEL} nobody will ever see; the sluice drips while you think
beats.g_k4: the tin patches gleam and the {G_WHEEL} turns a full week, long enough to feel like a winner, and then the sluice pounds one patch loose, then another, and the shed tin swims down and chokes the grating on the very morning of the full test
beats.g_k5: oak is stubborn and honest: all summer you learn to split it true, shape it with the drawknife, and set each paddle so the sluice water lands flat and full; the bells wait, the bet goes to {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} by default, and your hands grow carpenter-hard
beats.g_l1: the bell chamber becomes your map room: every cam chalk-numbered, every hammer measured, ropes coiled and tagged; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} voices bells across the scaffold, and his third bell rings so sweet you both stop working just to listen to it die away
beats.g_l2: the missing quarter-bell cam turns up in a biscuit tin, of course, but the real trouble hides lower: {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s bells ring beautifully and always four beats late, and the fault is neither bells nor cams but the escapement between {G_WHEEL} and chime, the clock's heartbeat itself
beats.g_l3: inside the housing you find a forty-year-old bodge: someone replaced the {G_ANCHOR} with a filed-down wrong part that has been limping ever since; a true {G_ANCHOR} must be made new, and {G_ANCHOR}-making, {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} says quietly, is master-level filing she has watched grown inventors fail
beats.g_l4: the castings cupboard holds three rough {G_ANCHOR} blanks, and you have evenings and stubbornness enough to file one secretly and surprise everyone, if your hands are as good as you hope; or you can ask {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} to teach you {G_ANCHOR}-filing properly, three slow weeks of lessons in front of everyone, with {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} watching every stroke
beats.g_l5: night filing goes wrong the way tired hands always do: the first blank goes under-square on Tuesday, the second chips on Thursday, and past midnight on Friday {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} finds you asleep at the bench, cheek on the third and last blank
beats.g_l6: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} does not scold; she puts the last blank back in the cupboard, walks you home under streetlights, and says the strongest thing an inventor ever makes is the sentence I need help; the {G_ANCHOR} will wait for autumn lessons
beats.g_m1: three weeks of lessons: file, breathe, check the square, file again; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} drifts over nightly to watch and stays to learn, trading his bell-voicing tricks for your filing strokes, until {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} starts calling the pair of you the tower crew like it is one word
beats.g_m2: the finished {G_ANCHOR} drops into the escapement like it grew there, and the clock breathes: tick, tock, steady as a sleeping cat, the first true beat in twenty years; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s bells come in four beats early now, which is to say, exactly on time
beats.g_m3: festival eve, and {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} reveals what {ROUTE_G_EVENT} actually is: at noon one inventor winds the great weight, one voices the bells, and one keeps the {G_WHEEL}, three posts held once in a lifetime; the guild asks the tower crew how tomorrow's noon will be crewed, and leaves the answer to you
beats.g_m4: three ways to stand at the first true noon: take the winding post yourself with {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} on bells, the {G_ANCHOR}-maker setting her own heartbeat going; hand {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} the winding and conduct the whole hour from the tower rail, timing bells, {G_WHEEL}, and seasons together; or slip your untested quarter-chime surprise, finished last night, into the noon program and make history twice
beats.g_m5: you set your palms to the great {G_KEY} as the square fills; below, the {G_WHEEL} takes the water, above, {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} stands ready at his sweetest bell, and the {G_ANCHOR}, your {G_ANCHOR}, waits to measure out the town's first honest noon in twenty years
beats.g_m6: {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} takes the winding post with shaking hands and a grin you have never seen on him; you climb to the tower rail with the pocket watch and the cue sheet, one eye on the {G_WHEEL} gauge, one on the bells, the whole clock laid out below you like an orchestra
beats.g_m7: your quarter-chime surprise, a ninth hammer rigged overnight to trill between the hours, goes into the noon program on a hope and a wire; there was no time to test it, and standing at the cam you can feel exactly how much no time there was
beats.g_z1: {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} sweeps the glitter into an envelope and labels it lesson one: hurry is hard on steel; there is no time left to forge another before {ROUTE_G_EVENT}, so the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER} watches the festival from the workshop window while the other three figures parade without it; on the last day of summer {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} hands you the envelope and a winter invitation to the forge, because spring-makers, she says, are made of exactly this kind of mistake
beats.g_z2: by {ROUTE_G_EVENT} you have not seen the tower or the {G_WHEEL} cellar even once, but every spring in all four figures is yours, and when the parade finally runs on festival day each leap and wingbeat lands true; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} introduces you to the crowd as the guild's new keeper of springs, a title nobody planned and nobody else earned; you came to fix a clock and instead discovered the one small thing you may be best at in the whole guild
beats.g_z3: three seasons parade at {ROUTE_G_EVENT} while winter rides in a padded crate to the city foundry; the crowd cheers anyway, but you know the gap in the parade by heart; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} finds you afterward and says, not unkindly, that he cracked a crown gear his first summer too, which is why he bought the press; you spend your last week drafting the {G_FIGURE_WINTER}'s rebuild plan for autumn, and {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} signs it approved with her red pencil
beats.g_z4: the four seasons parade every hour on {ROUTE_G_EVENT} day, dry basin and all, and nobody under ten cares even slightly about the missing water; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} announces the {G_DRAIN_GATE} hunt as next summer's first project and posts the sign-up sheet with your name already at the top, {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s second; twenty years of silence, she tells the crowd, ended because two juniors learned to share one press; the {G_FIGURE_SUMMER} leaps on the hour, every hour, all summer long
beats.g_z5: the {G_FIGURE_SPRING}, {G_FIGURE_SUMMER}, {G_FIGURE_AUTUMN}, and {G_FIGURE_WINTER} parade over rushing, returning water while eight bells ring and the whole square sings the Rewinding song; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} engraves two names on the maintenance plate inside the pedestal, yours and {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s, where only future menders will ever read them, which she says is exactly the right audience; you started the summer as the toothbrush kid and ended it as the junior who woke the seasons
beats.g_z6: from inside the pedestal the parade is all sound: the drain roaring at your crank, gears taking up, the crowd gasping somewhere above, {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} laughing like a much younger kid; afterward he cannot stop saying it should have been you, and you cannot explain that it was, that turning the hidden crank taught you what {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} meant all summer: half of every machine, and most of every guild, is the part that works out of sight
beats.g_z7: the verdict is nobody's favorite: the {G_WHEEL} must come out for a full rebuild come autumn, and {ROUTE_G_EVENT} runs on the emergency hand-wind, juniors taking turns at a crank in the cellar while the bells ring above; your turn comes at noon exactly, which {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} calls fitting; you spend the last weeks drawing the rebuild plan, tin envelope pinned to the corner as a warning, and the guild votes it the best-documented failure of the year
beats.g_z8: on the last day of summer the {G_WHEEL} turns true under full sluice for the first time in sixty years, so smooth you can balance a coin on the frame, and it will turn that way for sixty more; the town miller comes down to watch, runs his palm along your paddles, and offers you winter work at the mill on the spot; you never rang a bell all summer, and somehow found the trade your hands were built for
beats.g_z9: {ROUTE_G_EVENT} gets its chimes anyway, hand-rung: you in the bell chamber with a mallet, a pocket watch, and {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} calling the beats, ringing noon so nearly perfect that only the two of you know the clock itself stayed silent; the town hears bells for the first time in twenty years and dances; you hear the four-beat gap where your {G_ANCHOR} should have been, and sign up for autumn filing lessons before the last bell fades
beats.g_z10: noon arrives through your own hands: the weight rises, the {G_ANCHOR} beats, eight bells ring in perfect step while the four seasons parade below and the whole town sings the Rewinding song louder than the chimes; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} engraves the tower crew's names on the maintenance plate and declares the clock alive for another lifetime; on the way down {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} asks what you will restore next summer, and the wonderful truth is you have absolutely no idea
beats.g_z11: your countdown brings {G_WHEEL}, weight, bells, and seasons in together like sections of a band, and noon rings so exactly true that the station master checks his railway watch against the tower instead of the other way round; {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} shouts your name from the winding post for the whole square to hear; timing the team, {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} says over cocoa, is timekeeping, and she should know, she has been doing it to the pair of you all summer
beats.g_z12: the surprise cam skips at the worst moment, tangles the quarter-bell rope, and noon limps in four minutes late while you untie knots sixty feet above the laughing, kindly crowd; {ROUTE_G_MENTOR}'s verdict is one sentence, test before showtime, pinned above your bench forever; you post the fix by nightfall, ring a perfect private noon at midnight for an audience of {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} and two pigeons, and the guild adds your honest fix-note to its book of famous four-minute mistakes
beats.l_a1: the spiral stair ends in wind: the rooftop aero workshop is a long shed of balsa smell and wing ribs, wind socks snapping on every corner; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR}, an old airship rigger with weather-carved hands, clips a harness line to your belt before hello and teaches you the roof's first law, clip before you step
beats.l_a2: {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} walks you along the parapet and points out the derby course: a {L_RAIL} on the guild roof, three hoops rigged over three rooftops, and the landing lawn far below in the park; at summer's end {ROUTE_L_EVENT} decides the year's best flier, and your bench inherits a half-wrecked {L_TRAINER} with someone else's initials on the keel
beats.l_a3: a grey blur drops out of the sun and lands on the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} rail: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}, champion message pigeon, seven summers unbeaten, followed by his person, {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}, who reads your face and decides you are not competition yet; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} says the roof teaches two crafts, wings you build and wings you train, and every junior must pick a first love
beats.l_b1: two summer roads leave this rooftop: rebuild the battered trainer rib by rib until it can fly the derby course, a builder's road of glue, clamps, and test hops; or join the pigeon post, take on a bird of your own, and fly the post routes against {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} all summer, a trainer's road of dawn launches and weather; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} is pretending not to listen
beats.l_c1: you strip the {L_TRAINER} to its keel like a fish to the bone, numbering every rib with a stub of pencil; under the torn skin the old girl is honest wood, and {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} nods over your shoulder saying she flew the derby twice before either of you was born
beats.l_c2: the wind tunnel is a crate, a borrowed box fan, and wool streamers taped to the wing, and it tells the truth anyway: the streamers dance smooth along the middle and snarl into knots at both wingtips; the old wing stalls at the tips, which {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} says is what finally grounded her
beats.l_c3: {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} answers the tip stall with two drawings pinned side by side, a washout twist built into both wingtips, and two honest derby philosophies: a long {L_SOARER} that rides the high line above the chimneys, slow, patient, and certain of the distance; or a short {L_DART} for the fast line under the rooftop hoops, quick and twitchy and thrilling if you can hold it; the {L_TRAINER}'s keel will take either, exactly once
beats.l_f1: long wings need long spars, and long spars mean scarf joints, thin wedges of spruce glued and clamped overnight in a jig you build twice before it is straight; the shed smells of glue for a week and your fingertips wear little shells of it
beats.l_f2: off the low shed roof the {L_SOARER} simply refuses to come down, floating the length of the guild in one breath, but she wanders like a daydream, drifting left of wherever you aim her; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} diagnoses a missing rudder trim tab, a small part with strong opinions, and the derby trials are tomorrow
beats.l_f3: a wing this long wants two more hands at the {L_RAIL}: a tail-line crew to steady her through launch and a voice to call the drift at the hoops; but the derby trials paint only the pilot's name on the board, and asking {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} to crew means handing your build's every secret to your rival a month before the derby; the trials fly tomorrow at dawn, with or without you deciding out loud
beats.l_f4: solo at dawn, no one on the tail line and no voice calling the drift: the {L_SOARER} leaves the {L_RAIL} crooked, floats the first hoop anyway, then wanders wide of the second with your hands too full to correct, and settles herself, gentle and unbothered, into the market's striped awning like a cat into a hammock
beats.l_f5: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} says yes before you finish asking, and drafts a junior from the gadget shop for gust duty; the first crewed launch leaves the {L_RAIL} straight as a ruled line, and with a voice on the tail rope calling the drift, the {L_SOARER}'s famous wander shrinks to a whisper by the second morning
beats.l_c4: the crewed month is the best flying of the summer: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} calls the drift from the tail line, the gadget shop junior times the gusts, and between launches the three of you tune the {L_SOARER}'s trim tab until she flies a chalk line the length of the roof; other fliers start drifting over to ask about their own wandering wings
beats.l_g1: the {L_DART} comes together short-coupled and eager, a wing that answers before you finish asking; on the bench she is perfect, but the fast line under the hoops leaves no room for a sloppy start, and {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} says everything about the {L_DART}'s derby will be decided by the {L_RAIL} release
beats.l_g2: the only release hook that never misfires on this roof is {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}'s own design, guild-registered with her name stamped in the brass; you could ask to borrow the pattern, which means asking {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} for help, or puzzle out a copy from watching it work, which means not quite asking; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} raises one eyebrow and leaves the ethics to you
beats.l_g3: the copy would be easy, that is the trouble: two evenings with calipers while {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} flies {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}, and no one the wiser unless it slips; asking her straight out costs pride you have been saving all summer, and she might just laugh; the {L_DART} waits on the {L_RAIL}, hookless, while you decide who you are going to be about this
beats.l_g4: your copy is nearly right, which on a launch hook means wrong: at the trials it lets go a half-beat early, the {L_DART} noses off the {L_RAIL}, and she plants herself in the straw bales below the parapet, splintered but whole, while the field flies on without you
beats.l_g5: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} appears while you are still picking straw off the wreck, arms crossed, and does not say what she could; she says her first hook misfired for a whole spring before she got it right, and that the real pattern is yours next season, all you ever had to do was ask
beats.l_h1: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} says yes so fast you suspect she was waiting to be asked, and names her price: evening laps helping train {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} while she rebuilds her own entry; the true hook fits the {L_DART}'s {L_RAIL} like it grew there, and your launches go from lottery to clockwork in a week
beats.l_h2: mid-summer, a green-black storm tears across the town in an hour and takes the {L_RAIL} with it, peeling it off the parapet and dropping the flagpole through the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} roof for good measure; no {L_RAIL}, no derby, and the guild goes quiet the way a roof does when the wind stops
beats.l_h3: in the wreck you see it: the fallen flagpole is straight-grained and true, long enough to build not one {L_RAIL} but two side by side, a {L_CATAPULT} launch like the old airship slips used; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} turns your sketch over twice and says the words every junior waits for, bold, and possible
beats.l_h4: the guild will back one rebuild before derby day: restore the single {L_RAIL} exactly as it was, safe, proven, and done with a week to spare for practice; or build your {L_CATAPULT} from the storm's own flagpole, a faster launch than this roof has ever had, finished, if nothing slips, the night before the derby with no full test flight; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} votes with her eyebrows for the {L_CATAPULT}
beats.l_h5: you choose the sure road and build it well: the single {L_RAIL} goes back straighter than the storm found it, and the saved week becomes practice, dawn after dawn through the hoops until the {L_DART}'s line is grooved into your hands
beats.l_h6: derby day runs like your practice mornings with bunting on: launch, hoop, hoop, hoop, lawn, the {L_DART} flying her line like water finding a channel while the crowd on the street below tracks her shadow between the buildings
beats.l_i1: the {L_CATAPULT} build eats the roof: sawhorses, pole sections, {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} running {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} across town for hardware while shopkeepers gather in the street to watch the twin rails rise against the sky; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} checks every bolt behind you, twice, and teaches you to like it
beats.l_i2: dawn tests, goggles down: the {L_CATAPULT} throws the {L_DART} off the roof like a swift leaving a barn, faster than any launch this roof has seen, and your practice runs start beating times the guild board has kept under glass for thirty years
beats.l_i3: derby day, and the mayor herself waves the flag: the {L_CATAPULT} gleams, the hoops snap their pennants over three rooftops, and {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} checks your harness twice without a word; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}, first on the {L_RAIL} crew by her own demand, holds up the release and waits on your call
beats.l_i4: the {L_CATAPULT} gives you speed to spend and one decision left to make in the air's own language: fly the fast line, low under the chimneys where the record lives and the gusts bite between the buildings; or fly the high line over the weathervanes where no junior has hunted, riding whatever the morning sky is hiding up there
beats.l_i5: you call the fast line and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}'s release is perfect: the {L_DART} drops into the chimney canyon flat-out, hoops flicking past like page turns, the whole town's rooftops unrolling a foot below her keel
beats.l_i6: you call the high line and climb; over the weathervanes the morning has been keeping a secret, a smooth river of rising air no chart on the guild wall has ever shown, and the {L_DART} settles into it like a leaf onto a stream
beats.l_i7: the river of warm air carries the {L_DART} over the last hoop, over the landing lawn, over hedges and one very surprised picnic, while far behind you the derby crowd cranes and points at a glider doing what no chart on the guild wall says a glider should
beats.l_j1: the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} is a small warm city of twelve guild pigeons, route maps pinned three deep, and one wall of brass leg-capsules polished like trophies; at the center hangs the post board with {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}'s record, seven summers, never beaten home, and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} dusts that corner of the board personally
beats.l_j2: {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} hands you {L_PIGEON}, a small slate-grey hen with quick eyes who circles the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} twice before every landing, deciding; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} laughs out loud at her, calls her the ditherer; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} waits until {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} is gone and says quietly that the circling is not dithering, it is thinking, and thinking birds last
beats.l_j3: the Post Trials close the summer, three legs flown for keeps: the {L_SPRINT_LEG}, short and savage; the river cross, wide and windy; and the {L_STORM_LEG}, home through worsening weather before the last bell, the leg that makes legends; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} announces to the whole {ROUTE_L_LOFT} that {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} will fly all three, and looks at you when she says it
beats.l_j6: your first post week is all routes and rhythms: you walk the three great legs on foot with a map, the {L_SPRINT_LEG} cobbles, the wide river cross, the storm-bell line over Mill Hill, while {L_PIGEON} rides your shoulder and marks each landmark with one thoughtful circle overhead; by week's end you know every rooftop, bridge, and bell between the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} and the hills, and {L_PIGEON} knows them one circle better
beats.l_j4: a young bird can be made ready for one great leg, not three, and {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} makes you choose {L_PIGEON}'s summer: train her for speed, dawn sprints up Baker's Row against the clock until the {L_SPRINT_LEG} is hers to argue; or train her weather-sense, reading pressure and sky and shelter ledges, aiming her whole young heart at the {L_STORM_LEG}
beats.l_k1: sprint summer: every dawn you and {L_PIGEON} work Baker's Row, {ROUTE_L_LOFT} to market cross and home, and every dusk you log her times; she is not fast yet, but she is faster than yesterday, every single day, and her two thinking circles before landing get shorter as she gets surer
beats.l_k2: the last seconds hide in the {L_CAPSULE}: the regulation brass message {L_CAPSULE} is a small anchor on a small bird, and the {ROUTE_L_LOFT}'s old-timers whisper about featherweight capsules, paper-thin shells that are strictly borderline under post rules; one sits in your palm now, light as a beech leaf, entirely unweighed by any judge yet
beats.l_k3: the featherweight could be the wingbeat between {L_PIGEON} and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} in the {L_SPRINT_LEG}, and it might pass the judges' scale, and it might not, and you would fly the trials knowing which word you bet on; the regulation {L_CAPSULE} is honest weight, honest time, and trusts everything to the training you and {L_PIGEON} actually did; she watches you weigh her fates, head tilted
beats.l_k4: {L_PIGEON} flies the {L_SPRINT_LEG} of her young life, first home by a full three wingbeats over {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}, and the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} explodes, and then the judges' scale comes out at the finish table the way it always does at records, and the featherweight {L_CAPSULE} weighs exactly what you feared
beats.l_k5: regulation brass it is, and the last week of training becomes craft instead of tricks: you file the {L_CAPSULE} mount smoother, teach {L_PIGEON} a tighter line around the chimney at Baker's corner, and shave her landing circles from two to one; what you cannot make lighter you make cleaner
beats.l_l1: storm-school for a small grey hen: you teach {L_PIGEON} the pressure drop before rain, the shelter ledges on the mill and the church tower, the home line with the wind behind and the wind across; her double circle stops looking like doubt and starts looking like what it is, a bird reading the whole sky before committing
beats.l_l2: the secret you learn by accident: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}, the unbeatable, hates thunder, and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} has been hiding it for seven summers, flying him early, flying him around weather, guarding the record from the sky itself; you see it in one rehearsal flash, {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} flinching off his line and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}'s face going carefully blank
beats.l_l3: then the ruling: after a near-miss in the great August squall, {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} announces the {L_STORM_LEG} will be cancelled as too dangerous, unless someone proves a safe-flight protocol before trials day; the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} looks at its boots; the {L_STORM_LEG} is the leg {L_PIGEON} was born for, and the clock on proving it starts now
beats.l_l4: two ways to save the {L_STORM_LEG}: take {L_PIGEON} up in the next real squall and prove the protocol solo, one bird, one junior, and the rope-crew rule quietly bent, glory and the fix in one flight; or bring {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} in, pool what your weather-school knows with what her seven summers know, and design the protocol together, sharing the credit and telling her you have seen {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} flinch
beats.l_l5: the squall comes on a Thursday and you and {L_PIGEON} go up into it alone, no rope-crew, no spotter, just a junior on the parapet and a small bird reading a big sky; {L_PIGEON} flies it perfectly, ledge to ledge to home before the first bell stops swinging, and you land in trouble as deep as the weather
beats.l_l6: {ROUTE_L_MENTOR}'s anger is quiet and worse for it: the buddy rule is written in old rigger's blood, and no protocol is proven by breaking the rule it rides on; {L_PIGEON}'s flight data is good, so good it will be used, and you are grounded from the trials for a fortnight that contains them
beats.l_m1: you tell {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} everything, the protocol problem and the flinch you saw in August, and after one terrible silence she chooses to trust you back; your weather-school and her seven summers turn out to fit like gears: bell timings, shelter ledges, pressure calls, and a two-bird buddy system where a sky-reader leads and a strong flier holds the line
beats.l_m2: the guild adopts the protocol in a week: the {L_STORM_LEG} is saved, the trials confirmed, and {L_PIGEON} and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} become the {ROUTE_L_LOFT}'s first official buddy pair, the young hen teaching the old champion her double circle while he teaches her to punch a crosswind; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} starts eating lunch at your bench like it has always been that way
beats.l_m3: trials day saves its truth for the third leg: real weather walking in off the hills, pressure falling, the storm-bell rigged to ring; the postmaster rules the leg flies under your buddy protocol, two birds together, one leading, and leaves the naming of the lead to the two juniors who wrote it; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} looks at you and does not say {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}'s name
beats.l_m4: one storm leg, three ways to fly it: {L_PIGEON} leads, the sky-reader out front with {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}'s strength holding her flank, the protocol flown exactly as designed; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} leads, the old champion given his storm at last with {L_PIGEON} steadying the line behind, and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}'s seven summers honored in front of the whole guild; or jump the postmaster's bell and give the launch word early, ahead of the printed weather schedule your own protocol swears by, to chase the record crossing the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} has whispered about all week
beats.l_m5: {L_PIGEON} goes up first into the greening sky with {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} half a wingbeat back and the whole guild on the parapet under ropes and rules; her double circle at the mill ledge reads the squall's one safe seam, and she takes it, and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} follows her through
beats.l_m6: {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} goes up leading with {L_PIGEON} glued to his flank, and the old champion flies his forbidden weather at last, thunder and all, steadied every time he flinches by a small grey hen holding the line a wingbeat behind; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} watches her bird's whole secret become his finest flight
beats.l_m7: you give the launch word two minutes before the postmaster's bell, chasing a record, and the pair goes up into the very sky the printed schedule was written to avoid; the squall's worst seam arrives exactly where your own timetable said it would, and {L_PIGEON}'s double circle reads it and takes both birds to shelter on the mill ledge, protocol-perfect, waiting out the weather you launched them into
beats.l_z1: the judges are kind and the rules are rules: no second hoop, no derby seed, and the re-fly they grant you lands a month after summer ends; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} walks you home past the awning and says the only thing he ever says twice, big wings are crewed wings; the last weeks become crewed practice with {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} on the tail line, and the autumn re-fly goes clean through all three hoops with only the crew and the pigeons watching, which turns out to be plenty
beats.l_z2: the derby comes and the {L_SOARER} flies it mid-pack, steady as a ferry, and it does not matter at all: by then half the field launches with your crew calls and flies with your trim tabs, and every clean turn through the hoops is a little bit yours; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} enters the tab design and the tail-line drill in the guild book with your name in rigger's capitals and the title trim doctor underneath; you came to win a derby and instead discovered the quiet trade of making other people's wings honest, which {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} says was always the better secret
beats.l_z3: the {L_DART} sits out the derby getting a new nose while you crew the {L_RAIL} for the fliers who made the cut, learning every release on the roof from the working end; by summer's close you and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} have rebuilt the {L_DART} together around her true hook, and the last thing you do is chalk your trial number on her keel for next June; some summers, {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} says, you win the friend and the wing has to wait
beats.l_z4: three clean hoops and the fastest junior time of the day plants you second overall behind a guild senior twice your size and years, and first junior on the derby board in paint that {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} lets you stir; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}'s rebuilt entry takes third and she splits her victory toffee with you on the parapet, co-plotting openly for next summer; the trainer that came to you half-wrecked goes into winter storage with a ribbon on her keel
beats.l_z5: the fastest junior run ever recorded on this roof, and the derby cup says so, but the part you will keep forever is smaller: at your insistence the engraver adds a second line, {L_RAIL} crew, J. {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}, and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} pretends the wind is making her eyes water at the ceremony; {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} hangs the stopwatch around your neck and tells the guild the storm broke his {L_RAIL} and two juniors built him a better one
beats.l_z6: the feather landing in the long meadow is the farthest flight in guild memory, and the judges regretfully rule you out for distance; disqualified, and the only flier who came home with a discovery: {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} starts a new wind map that evening with the thermal river drawn in your name, and half the roof spends September learning to find it; records fade, he says, but a named wind is forever
beats.l_z7: the record is scratched from the book while the ink is still wet, and {L_PIGEON}'s great sprint becomes an exhibition flight, applauded and uncounted; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} finds you at the empty finish table and says, oddly gently, that {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} was disqualified once too, ask him about his second summer sometime; you fly {L_PIGEON} home under the regulation {L_CAPSULE} that evening, three wingbeats slower and entirely yours, and start the winter list titled next year, by the rules
beats.l_z8: {L_PIGEON} takes second in the {L_SPRINT_LEG}, one honest wingbeat behind {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}, and her line home is so steady the postmaster stops the applause to ask whose bird that is; second place, and a signature: {L_PIGEON} joins the winter mail team on the spot, the youngest bird ever signed, and you spend the last week of summer building her cold-weather {L_CAPSULE} to the postmaster's own pattern; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} keeps the record, {L_PIGEON} gets the job
beats.l_z9: the {L_STORM_LEG} flies on trials day under a protocol built from {L_PIGEON}'s Thursday, adopted by the guild without your name on it, that being the price {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} names for the manner of its proving; you watch from the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} rail with {L_PIGEON} on your shoulder while other birds fly her route; in September the postmaster quietly adds a line to the protocol sheet, first flown by {L_PIGEON}, and {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} lets it stand, rules, he says, are owed to the living, credit to the honest
beats.l_z10: both birds beat the last bell home through the first true storm the trials have flown in nine years, and the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} goes up like a struck match; the postmaster enters the buddy protocol into the permanent post regulations and, at your suggestion, names it the {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} rule, at which {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} walks away fast and comes back red-eyed and punches your shoulder softly; two portraits go up over the post board that night, {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} and {L_PIGEON}, and under {L_PIGEON}'s the painter adds two small circles, so the story stays told
beats.l_z11: they come home together on the bell, {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} leading brave and {L_PIGEON} precise, and {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} says thank you out loud in front of the entire {ROUTE_L_LOFT}, which costs her more than the record ever did; the post board gets a new line under {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}'s seven summers, storm-flown at last, and the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} gets co-captains, one of whom is you; rivals, the postmaster observes, make middling stories, but a champion who learned to be caught, that one he will tell for years
beats.l_z12: the birds sit out the worst of the squall on the mill ledge, dry and thoroughly unimpressed, and glide home together long after the last bell has stopped swinging: no record, no scored crossing, two safe birds, and one junior who jumped a bell that existed for a reason; the postmaster reads you the schedule's small print, which turns out to be in {ROUTE_L_MENTOR}'s handwriting, and {ROUTE_L_MENTOR} adds the lesson underneath, the timing was the invention; {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} flies the make-up crossing with you a week later, by the book, and the book, you admit out loud, flies better
beats.m_a1: the gadget shop is the guild's front tooth, opening right onto {STREET_NAME}: a wall of little labeled drawers, hooks of half-finished helpers, and {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} in an apron of forty pockets; her first law goes up over your bench in her own handwriting, ask the shopkeeper twice, build once
beats.m_a2: the street is a parade of solvable troubles: {M_BAKER} the baker fights his dawn rush up a narrow stair, forty loaves at a time; {M_GREENGROCER}'s beautiful tilted apple display sheds apples down the cobbles every time the tram rumbles past; and at summer's end {ROUTE_M_EVENT} takes over the whole street, ribbons and all, judged on helpers that truly help
beats.m_a3: {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s bench is a small museum: labeled tools, squared drawings, edges you could shave by; she has entered the fair twice and never won, and her handshake says she does not intend to lose to the new junior; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} posts the summer ledger, every inventor adopts one shopkeeper, and your fair entry must grow out of that real work
beats.m_b1: two names are left unclaimed in the ledger and {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} holds the pen out to you: {M_BAKER}, whose dawn rush is a four-in-the-morning war between forty loaves, one narrow stair, and a bell that never rings on time; or {M_GREENGROCER}, whose runaway apples have rolled so far down {STREET_NAME} that the fishmonger catches them in his hat; {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} signs for the fishmonger without looking up
beats.m_c1: four in the morning at {M_BAKER}'s bakery is weather of its own, flour fog and oven heat: you watch him haul basket after basket up the narrow stair while eight ovens tick to eight different clocks in his head, and you fill three notebook pages before the sun is even up
beats.m_c2: asked once, {M_BAKER} says the stair is the problem; asked twice, {ROUTE_M_MENTOR}'s rule working like a charm, he admits the truth: it is the timing, eight ovens proofing and baking on eight schedules with nothing but his memory; back at the bench your first {M_CLOCK_DEVICE} sketch collects one pointed correction from {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} in passing, your return springs will tire by August, which is irritating and, you realize by supper, correct
beats.m_c4: two builds could change {M_BAKER}'s dawn and there is summer enough for one done well: the {M_CLOCK_DEVICE} with eight weighted flags, springless thanks to {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s unwanted advice, the fix for the real problem he only admitted when asked twice; or a {M_LIFT_DEVICE} basket lift up the narrow stair, counterweights and pulleys from {ROUTE_M_MENTOR}'s crates, the fix for the problem everyone can see
beats.m_f1: {M_CLOCK_DEVICE} version one is cuckoo chaos: flags drop in pairs, flour packs the works, and one morning the whole dial spins backward to {M_BAKER}'s enormous delight; you learn to case the works against flour the way {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} cases everything on this street, twice
beats.m_f2: version two, weighted flags riding sealed brass tubes, runs two ovens for a full week without a miss, and {M_BAKER} stops checking those two ovens at all, which he has not done in thirty years; six ovens to go, and the fair is suddenly close enough to smell the bunting
beats.m_f3: the fair asks its question: scale version two up to all eight ovens in one heroic fortnight, a full bakery clock on the fair stage with {M_BAKER} himself demonstrating, glory or tangle; or perfect the two-oven clock you know is true, enter it small and finished, and let eight ovens wait for autumn; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR}, asked for advice, taps her own first law and says nothing
beats.m_f5: you spend the fortnight making the small clock unarguable: sealed tubes polished, flags balanced to drop in half a heartbeat, and a little brass door for cleaning out flour that {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} inspects twice and pronounces sensible, which from {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} is a parade
beats.m_f6: the last days go to ovens three and four at the bakery itself, the clock earning its keep in real flour weather while {M_BAKER} learns to trust it, plus a hand-lettered card explaining the whole trick; it is the least spectacular entry on your street of the fair, and the most finished thing you have ever made
beats.m_f4: the eight-flag clock is magnificent for exactly four minutes of fair morning, then flag six snags flag five, the tangle walks across the dial like knitting coming undone, and somewhere behind the stage {M_BAKER}'s actual bread, on the old system of his memory, quietly over-bakes
beats.m_g1: the {M_LIFT_DEVICE} starts as chalk lines on the bakery wall: a basket sized to {M_BAKER}'s trays, counterweights from {ROUTE_M_MENTOR}'s crates, pulleys trued on your own bench; the narrow stair measures narrower every visit, and the basket clears it by the width of two fingers, if nothing swings
beats.m_g2: the test lift glides up like a dream and comes down like an anvil: a loaded basket needs a brake, a real one, and the finest brake design in the guild lives in {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s locked drawer, built for the fair entry she guards like a dragon; she catches you looking at the drawer and slides it shut with her elbow
beats.m_g3: an old millwright's book in the guild library shows a rope-twist brake you could improvise by Friday, diagrams smudged, margins full of some dead tinkerer's warnings; or there is the door you have not tried, offering {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} a straight trade, your clean gear-cutting, which she has watched sideways for weeks, for her brake; the basket waits at the top of the stair, wedged
beats.m_h1: the trade lands: your gear-cutting for her brake, working side by side at her spotless bench, where you learn she checks everything three times because her first fair entry failed in front of everyone; her brake plus your gears makes the lift glide up and settle down soft as a yawn, fully loaded
beats.m_h2: triumph lasts one day: the health inspector, making fair-season rounds, rules that no rope lift may run beside food without a guard cage, and posts the notice with three days left before the fair; {M_BAKER} offers to hide the lift till September, and {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} surprises you both by saying no, we fix it
beats.m_h3: one long evening in {ROUTE_M_MENTOR}'s mesh drawers turns up the answer: a folding cage of baker's cooling mesh that latches shut around the basket's path and folds flat for cleaning, {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} drawing while you cut, {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} appearing at exactly the right moments with exactly the right hinge, the way she always somehow does
beats.m_h5: you bolt the caged lift into the bakery in the dark, and dawn arrives like a held breath: baskets rise, the brake sighs, the inspector initials the cage latch, and {M_BAKER} walks up his own stair empty-handed for the first time in thirty years, laughing all the way
beats.m_h6: word travels the way warm bread does: the dawn rush halves, the extra hour becomes a new seeded loaf named after you, and on judging day the fair judges leave their tent and walk to the bakery to watch the helper actually helping, which none of them can remember doing before
beats.m_g4: the rope-twist brake judders like a nervous laugh, holds through every empty test, and then meets its first real load of morning rolls; the basket sails down the stairwell in stately slow disaster and forty rolls take {STREET_NAME} at a gallop, pursued by pigeons
beats.m_g5: nobody is hurt and the ducks in the fountain eat like kings, but {M_BAKER} retires the lift on the spot until it earns a proper brake, and he is right; that evening you find {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s brake drawings on your bench, unasked, weighted down with one of her perfect gears and no note at all
beats.m_j1: {M_GREENGROCER}'s stall is the prettiest physics problem on {STREET_NAME}: a tilted display that shows every apple to the sun, and every time the tram rumbles past, the top row breaks loose and rolls for the harbor; she catches two, the fishmonger's hat catches one, and {STREET_NAME} eats the rest; she has lost a crate a week all spring
beats.m_j2: asked once, she says stop the apples; asked twice, she stops weighing and tells the truth of her trade: the tilt is the shop, people buy apples they can see blushing, and every flat safe tray she has tried sold half; whatever you build must keep the tilt and keep the apples, both, or it is no helper at all
beats.m_j3: back at the shop, {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} unveils the fair's new crown: {M_FAIR_EVENT}, a judged morning where every helper runs live at its own real stall, the whole street working at once; {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} is deep in a descaling machine for the fishmonger, muttering at fish grit, and the row will put your stall and hers side by side on fair morning, working or not
beats.m_j5: you spend the week measuring instead of making: the tilt's exact angle, the top row's tipping point, the tram timetable copied into your notebook, and a chalk mark on the cobbles where the rumble hits hardest; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} calls it the most invisible work an inventor does and the only kind that never gets rebuilt
beats.m_j4: two inventions could save the apples and there is summer enough to master one: a {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} under the stall's legs, springs and sand-pots tuned to drink the tram's shudder before it ever reaches the fruit; or an {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} along the display's lip, with a felt-lined return chute that rolls runaways gently home to the bottom of the tilt; the tram bell rings the quarter hour like a taunt
beats.m_k1: {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} school: you build spring-and-sand-pot feet on the workshop floor and shake the bench to the tram timetable, tuning each pot until a rumble goes in and a shiver comes out; on the floor of the shop it works every time, and the floor of the shop, {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} observes, is not {STREET_NAME}
beats.m_k2: the cobbles are the true exam: the same {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} that sits stone-calm on the workshop floor shivers differently on stone, {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} warns, and only a real tram on the real street will tell you which sand-pot is lying; street testing, though, is a permitted thing, with a form and a stamp and a clerk who cannot be hurried
beats.m_k6: the clerk's calendar says one week for the stamp, which leaves two bare days between permit and {M_FAIR_EVENT}, enough to tune four sand-pots if nothing surprises you; or there is the other way, wheeling the stall out under the gas lamps tonight and testing against the late trams with nobody's leave asked, data by dawn, and the nightwatch walks that beat twice an hour
beats.m_k3: the night test would settle every spring and sand-pot this week, with days to spare, and the guild's street rights are not yours to gamble, and both of those things are true at once; the permit path is honest and slow and leaves you two bare days of tuning before the row, betting the summer on a week you cannot spend building; the last tram of the night rumbles past the shop window like a dare
beats.m_k5: the lost week becomes the found week: while the permit crawls, you park a jar of dried peas on {M_GREENGROCER}'s counter and chart every tram's rumble by how hard the peas dance, morning to closing, until you know the street's shudder better than the tram drivers do; the permit arrives with two days to tune, and your {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} is tuned to data nobody else has
beats.m_k4: the {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} drinks its first real tram beautifully at half past eleven, and the nightwatch rounds the corner at twenty to twelve; what follows involves lanterns, a ledger, {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} arriving in her dressing gown to vouch for you, and the guild's street-testing rights suspended for the season by a sergeant who is very sorry and does it anyway
beats.m_l1: the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} rises along the display's lip: a slim brass {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} that lets buyers reach in but not apples out, feeding a canvas return chute that rolls escapees home to the bottom of the tilt; version one works perfectly and bruises every apple it saves, which {M_GREENGROCER} points out makes it a very tidy cider press
beats.m_l2: felt is the cure, glued in strips down the chute until a dropped apple lands like a secret; {M_GREENGROCER}'s daughter tests it by juggling over the display at closing time, and the chute forgives everything she throws; two stalls down, {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s descaling machine jams again on fish-scale grit, and her muttering has gone quiet, which from {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} is the bad sign
beats.m_l3: two weeks to {M_FAIR_EVENT}: your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} is done and drilled, apples rolling home twenty-nine times in thirty, while {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s scaler eats itself daily and she files the same jammed part over and over, too proud to ask the junior who beat her to a finished entry; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} says nothing at all in a way that is extremely loud
beats.m_l4: the contest math is simple and cold: every hour at {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s stall is an hour your own entry is not being polished, and {M_FAIR_EVENT} hands out exactly one first ribbon; you could spend the last fortnight making the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} unbeatable, drilling the demo until no judge can look away; or you could knock on the bench of the girl who has never once asked for help, carrying your felt scraps and your chute tricks, and find out what happens to contests then
beats.m_l5: you polish until the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} cannot miss: thirty for thirty, then fifty, the felt renewed, the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} buffed blinding, the demo patter tight as a drum; two stalls down the muttering stops one afternoon and does not start again, and you keep your eyes on your own beautiful {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} and tell yourself the row is a contest
beats.m_l6: {M_FAIR_EVENT} morning: {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s stall stands dark under a folded tarp, withdrawn, the fishmonger back to scaling by hand with a shrug that forgives nobody in particular; your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} runs fifty for fifty past a judges' table that keeps glancing at the gap in the row where a scaler should have been singing
beats.m_m1: {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} opens her jammed scaler wordlessly, which is how you know how bad it is: fish-scale grit has eaten the slide your whole chute trick was born to fix, and your felt-and-canvas lining fits her machine like it was always missing; in trade she re-cuts your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE}'s {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} brackets so true the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} rings when you tap it
beats.m_m2: rehearsal morning, the whole street runs at once: apples rolling home, fish scaling clean, {M_BAKER}'s bell ringing on time, a dozen small helpers humming at a dozen stalls, and {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} walks the length of {M_FAIR_EVENT} slow as church, saying nothing, smiling like the street is a machine she built herself, which, you realize, it rather is
beats.m_m3: fair morning: the mayor at the head of the row with the bell rope in hand, judges clipboards ready, {M_GREENGROCER} arranging her bravest tilt of the summer; she asks where you will stand when the bell goes, and at the next stall {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} tightens her last bolt, looks over at you, and leaves the question sitting in the sun between the two of you
beats.m_m4: the bell rope rises and three places call you at once: {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s stall, co-running the scaler you helped save, two names on one machine at the fair that broke her heart twice; your own stall, running the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} for the judges at {M_GREENGROCER}'s side, the summer's work shown by its maker; or the head of the row by the mayor's bell, where every visitor passes first, if you hurry your stall up there now, trading {M_GREENGROCER}'s real corner, tilt, tram, and all, for a borrowed flat table in the best spot on the street
beats.m_m5: you take your place at {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s side and feed the scaler its first fair-morning fish as the bell fades; the felt-lined slide sheds grit like rain off canvas, the machine sings the whole morning through, and two stalls down you can hear, every quarter hour, the soft thunder of your own {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} rolling apples home to no one
beats.m_m6: the bell rings, the first tram of the morning rumbles down {STREET_NAME} on schedule, and the judges reach {M_GREENGROCER}'s stall just as the top row of apples breaks loose in the sun exactly the way it has all summer; the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} and the chute do the rest while you narrate nothing at all
beats.m_m7: you talk the row steward into the head-of-row spot and rebuild the display on a borrowed flat table in twenty rushed minutes, skipping the asked-twice question you of all juniors should have asked: prime position, biggest crowds, and a {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} guarding apples that, on level boards far from the tram's rumble, have not the slightest intention of rolling anywhere
beats.m_z1: no ribbon, and the right stranger: the flour mill's engineer stands at your small table for twenty entire minutes, works the flags, opens the cleaning door, reads the card twice, and orders three clocks for the mill floor before walking on; your first commission, written on the back of the engineer's calling card; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} frames the order slip over your bench and tells the whole shop that ribbons fade but repeat customers, dear, are forever
beats.m_z2: {M_BAKER} rescues the morning like the showman he secretly is, selling the dark tray as extra-crusty specials to a laughing crowd and feeding the judges the evidence; your demo becomes the honest two-oven clock, which works perfectly and wins polite applause and no ribbon; on the walk home he orders the eight-oven clock anyway, built slow over autumn, one oven a week, and {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} frames your fair-morning to-do list as a lesson titled ambition, with flour in it
beats.m_z3: the judges score the lift where it lives, between the flour fog and the cooling racks, and invent a ribbon on the spot, best helper at work, because none of their categories fit a machine that never came to the fair; the breakfast bun with your name sells out by eight for the rest of the summer, and {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s brake gets its own line on the award card at your insistence; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} adds a second law over your bench, the best booth, dear, is the shop itself
beats.m_z4: the lift waits for autumn and the brake it deserves, and your summer becomes the study of stopping: you build {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s brake at quarter scale, then half, filling a notebook with why each part is shaped the way it is, and mail her your gear templates in return; at the fair you demo the day it rained rolls as a cautionary exhibit that draws the biggest laugh of the afternoon, and {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} pins a card to it, every inventor owns one famous morning
beats.m_z5: on {M_FAIR_EVENT} morning the stall stands stone-still through six trams while the apples doze in their tilt, and the judges hand your {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} second place in the row; the surprise comes at noon, when a man from the tram company asks to buy not the {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} but the jar-of-peas chart, which maps every soft {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} joint on {STREET_NAME}; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} shelves a copy in the guild library under reference, your name on the spine, and teaches you the word that matters, the measuring, dear, was the invention
beats.m_z6: the {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} is real and the price was real too: with street rights gone for the season, {M_FAIR_EVENT} judges your invention on a cobble-box replica while {M_GREENGROCER}'s actual stall shivers untested a stone's throw away; honorable mention, with a written note about patience that {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} does not frame; she sets you instead to drafting the guild's first night-testing permit proposal for the town council, because the rule that caught you, she says, is a bad rule badly needed, and fixing rules is inventing too
beats.m_z7: your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} takes its stall's points and the row loses the ribbon it wanted: the judges' card praises your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} and notes, in small firm letters, that a helper street helps together, and the grand ribbon goes to a smaller row across town that ran complete; you carry home a certificate and a knot in your stomach that outlasts it, and in September you mail {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} your gear templates with a one-line note, next summer, both stalls; her answer is a drawing of your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE}, corrected, which is {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} for yes
beats.m_z8: {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s stall takes first in {M_FAIR_EVENT} and the judges read the card aloud, her name and then, because she wrote it there in her squarest lettering, yours; her thank-you speech is four words long, which for {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} is an oration, and {M_GREENGROCER} reports the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} ran perfectly all morning unattended, which {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} pronounces the highest compliment a helper can earn, working best, dear, when you are busy being elsewhere; the two of you spend the last week of summer drawing machines that need four hands on one page
beats.m_z9: forty apples roll home to a cheer that brings the whole row running, and {M_FAIR_EVENT} is scored the way it worked, as one machine the length of a street: the grand ribbon names every helper and every inventor, your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} and {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s scaler two lines apart on the same card, the way {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} always intended; {M_GREENGROCER} sells out by noon, tilt and all, and gives the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE}'s first saved apple a shelf of its own; the fair closes with the mayor asking {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} what {STREET_NAME} will build next year, and {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} looking down the row at her juniors and saying, ask them twice
beats.m_z10: the judges stop, watch your {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} guard forty perfectly motionless apples, and move on, because a helper shown away from its trouble has nothing to help with; back at her tram-side corner {M_GREENGROCER} catches runaways in her apron all morning, kind about it and slower every hour, and the scoring card notes one stall out of place; {ROUTE_M_MENTOR} walks you back down the row at noon, bolts the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} where it belonged, and the first apple it rolls home draws a bigger crowd than the head of the row ever did; next year's ledger entry, in your own writing, says judged where it helps

g_g3.c_g_g3_b: Take {ROUTE_G_RIVAL}'s trade and share the press.

g_i4.c_g_i4_a: Wind the clock yourself, as {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} offered.
g_i4.c_g_i4_b: Give {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} the {G_KEY} and take the hidden crank.

g_l4.c_g_l4_b: Ask {ROUTE_G_MENTOR} to teach you, three slow weeks.

g_m4.c_g_m4_a: Take the winding post, {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} on bells.
g_m4.c_g_m4_b: Give {ROUTE_G_RIVAL} the winding and conduct the hour.

l_b1.c_l_b1_b: Join the pigeon post and race {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL}.

l_c3.c_l_c3_a: Build the long {L_SOARER} for the high line.
l_c3.c_l_c3_b: Build the short {L_DART} for the fast line.

l_f1.c_l_f1: Take the {L_SOARER} up for a hop.

l_f3.c_l_f3_b: Ask {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} and a friend to crew the launch.

l_g3.c_l_g3_a: Rig a quiet copy of {ROUTE_L_RIVAL}'s hook.
l_g3.c_l_g3_b: Ask {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} straight out to share the release.

l_h4.c_l_h4_a: Rebuild the single {L_RAIL}, safe and proven.

l_k3.c_l_k3_a: Fly the featherweight {L_CAPSULE} in the trials.
l_k3.c_l_k3_b: Keep the regulation {L_CAPSULE} and trust the training.

l_l4.c_l_l4_b: Bring {ROUTE_L_RIVAL} in and design it together.

l_m4.c_l_m4_a: Let {L_PIGEON} lead the storm leg, {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} flanking.
l_m4.c_l_m4_b: Let {ROUTE_L_RIVAL_ANIMAL} lead, with {L_PIGEON} holding the line.

l_m7.c_l_m7: Wait at the {ROUTE_L_LOFT} rail for the sky to clear.

m_c4.c_m_c4_b: Build the {M_LIFT_DEVICE} for the narrow stair.

m_g3.c_m_g3_a: Offer {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} a trade for her brake design.

m_j4.c_m_j4_a: Build the {M_DAMPER_DEVICE} for the stall's legs.

m_l4.c_m_l4_a: Knock on {ROUTE_M_RIVAL}'s bench and offer your help.

m_m4.c_m_m4_a: Stand with {ROUTE_M_RIVAL} and co-run the scaler.
m_m4.c_m_m4_b: Run the {M_CATCHER_DEVICE} at your own stall.
